key_insight: A fault-tolerant, literature-grounded LLM pipeline can autonomously progress from large-corpus discovery to reproducible first-principles computations and manuscript writing in frontier computational physics.
engineering_relevance: The work suggests that reliable autonomous scientific agents for high-stakes materials research require fresh-context isolation, external literature anchoring, and checkpointed reproduction rather than relying on internal model priors alone.
method: Map a corpus of 11,083 recent condensed-matter arXiv papers to identify a research direction.
Use calibration checkpoints to reproduce published references before running novel first-principles computations.
Write the manuscript through 47 fresh-context sessions with shared on-disk state, grounded by 2,162 literature-consultation events.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an end-to-end run that produced three substantive physics findings and compares two ablations, with a pre-architecture baseline and a no-pilot variant used to isolate grounding mechanisms.
watch_item: The pipeline depends on substantial literature curation and reproduction checkpoints, so its robustness may be harder to transfer to domains with sparse or poorly documented reference benchmarks.
paper_title: Grounded autonomous research: a fault-tolerant LLM pipeline from corpus to manuscript in frontier computational physics
